D5 Design L2 Understand Atom

Rubin's Vase

a vase, or two faces? when figure and ground are equally light the brain can't decide — and keeps flipping

Vision must split every scene into figure and ground — what to attend to and what to ignore. Rubin's 1915 vase shares one contour between a vase and two profiles, so either can be the figure. The flip is most unstable when both regions are equally light, giving the brain no cue to choose.

Slide the centre lightness toward the surround. As the two regions converge, the figure-ground assignment loses its anchor and the image becomes genuinely bistable — swap the roles to nudge which reading you see first.
